rationale: In the theory section, the citing paper uses Erdem, Mayhew, and Sun (2001) as specific prior evidence that downside versus upside discrepancies can produce asymmetric effects. This citation is tied to the development of H2 on asymmetry in downside versus upside brand dispersion, not just a generic literature list. Although it is not a methodological base for the full study, it meaningfully informs the conceptual framing of the asymmetry prediction.
relation_description: The citing paper transfers the cited paper's asymmetry insight (stronger downside vs upside effects) to a new context, using it to motivate tests of downside and upside brand-dispersion effects on stock returns and risk.
